mappings.rationale: Darwin's theory of evolution is fundamental to the history of biology, transforming Aristotelian concepts of classification, heredity, and descent.
Evolution is the unifying theory of biology, directly relevant to the historical development of biological ideas about classification, reproduction, and heredity.
Covers the history of life on Earth, central to understanding the origins and development of biological ideas from ancient civilizations onward.
Developmental biology addresses reproduction and embryological concepts central to the history of biology from Aristotelian times through the Renaissance.
Cell biology is a foundational biological discipline whose history includes microscopy, cell theory, and modern molecular approaches to life.
The study of genes and heredity is part of the historical development of biological ideas about reproduction and descent mentioned in section A.1.b.
Heredity is explicitly mentioned in section A.1.b as part of Aristotelian biological concepts, and its study is a major thread in biology's history.
Genomics represents a modern development in the biological sciences, building on the historical development of genetics and molecular biology.
Molecular biology is a major branch of modern biological science that emerged from the historical trajectory of biology described in this section.
Stem cell research represents cutting-edge biological science, building on the historical development of cell biology and developmental biology.
Synthetic biology is a contemporary frontier of the biological sciences, extending the historical trajectory from classification to engineering of life.
Systems biology represents a modern integrative approach to biological sciences, building on the historical development of quantitative biology.
Ecology is a major branch of the biological sciences studying relationships between organisms and their environments.
Zoology as a separate discipline is mentioned in section A.1.c, and animal classification is part of the Aristotelian biological tradition (A.1.b).
Botanical investigations are mentioned in section A.1.b (Greco-Roman world) and A.1.d (Renaissance dissemination of botanical knowledge).
Fungi are a major kingdom of life whose study is part of the historical development of botany and biological classification.
Bacteriology is a major branch of the biological sciences that emerged with the development of microscopy and microbiology.
Virology is a major branch of the biological sciences, closely connected to the historical development of microbiology and cell biology.
Microbiology is a fundamental biological science whose history includes the development of microscopy and germ theory.
Microbiome research is a modern branch of the biological sciences studying microbial communities in biological systems.
Entomology is a branch of zoology, which developed as a separate biological discipline as discussed in section A.1.c.
Mammalogy is part of zoology, which developed as a biological discipline alongside botany as discussed in section A.1.c.
The study of reptiles is part of zoology, which developed as a separate biological discipline as discussed in section A.1.c.
The study of amphibians is part of zoology, which developed as a separate biological discipline as discussed in section A.1.c.
Paleontological study of dinosaurs represents a major chapter in the biological sciences and the history of life on Earth.
Marine biology is a major branch of the biological sciences studying life in ocean environments.
Animal behavior studies (ethology) are a major branch of the biological sciences extending from Aristotelian biological observations.
Sexual selection is a key mechanism of evolution, directly connected to the Darwinian tradition in the biological sciences.
Human evolution is a major topic in biological science, connecting classification, heredity, and descent discussed in section A.1.b.
Neuroscience is a major branch of biological science, connected to the historical development of anatomical discoveries mentioned in sections A.1.b-d.
The study of the eye connects to anatomy and comparative biology, branches of biological science with deep historical roots.
Immunology is a major branch of the biological sciences, studying the body's defense mechanisms at the cellular and molecular level.
Endocrinology is a branch of biological science studying hormonal regulation, connected to the history of physiology.
Enzyme biochemistry is fundamental to the biological sciences, connecting chemistry and biology at the molecular level.
Circadian biology is a branch of the biological sciences studying internal biological clocks and their regulation.
The study of teeth connects to comparative anatomy, evolution, and paleontology within the biological sciences.
Hematology is part of the biological sciences, connected to the historical study of physiology and anatomy.
Forest ecology is a major branch of biological science studying plant communities and ecosystem dynamics.
Coral reef biology is part of marine biology and ecology, major branches of the biological sciences.
Biodiversity conservation applies biological science principles to the preservation of species diversity and ecosystems.
The study of invasive species is a branch of ecology and conservation biology within the biological sciences.
Extinction studies combine paleontology, ecology, and evolutionary biology, all major branches of the biological sciences.
Biogeography studies the distribution of biological species, connecting ecology, evolution, and geography.
Symbiosis is a key concept in ecology and evolutionary biology, major branches of the biological sciences.
Astrobiology extends the biological sciences to the question of life beyond Earth, connecting to the origin and nature of life.
Microscopy has been essential to the development of biological sciences, enabling the study of cells, microorganisms, and anatomical structures.
Addresses philosophical questions about classification, evolution, and the nature of life that arise from the biological sciences.
Aristotle's concepts of classification, reproduction, heredity, and descent are specifically cited in section A.1.b as foundational to biology.
Section A.1.b covers biology in the Greco-Roman world, including theories about life's origin and early botanical and anatomical discoveries.
Eugenics represents a historical application and misapplication of biological ideas about heredity and descent.
Pavlov's experimental work in physiology contributed to the development of biological sciences and the study of animal behavior.
The biology of smell involves neuroscience, physiology, and sensory biology, all branches of the biological sciences.
Mathematical biology applies mathematical models to biological problems including epidemiology, ecology, and pattern formation — relevant to the methods of the biological sciences.
